title: Perform Maintenance
plainEnglish: The organization must actually perform maintenance on its systems — not just have a policy that says it will. Maintenance is broadly defined and includes corrective maintenance (repairing problems), preventative maintenance (updates to prevent potential problems), adaptive maintenance (changes to the operative environment), and perfective maintenance (improving operations). This covers all system components: hardware, firmware, applications, and even ancillary devices like printers, scanners, and copiers. The Assessment Guide notes that regular patching is one common form but is not the only form. The single objective is that maintenance is performed — assessors look for records showing that maintenance actually happens on a regular, documented basis.

objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that maintenance is actually performed — maintenance records, patch logs, work orders, vendor service records, or change management tickets showing maintenance activities completed on organizational systems. Assessors look for a pattern of regular, documented activity rather than one-off records. The records should cover the four types of maintenance: corrective, preventative, adaptive, and perfective. A maintenance policy alone does not satisfy this objective — it must be backed by execution records.
commonEvidence: Maintenance records and work orders showing completed maintenance activities
Patch management logs showing patch deployment history
Vendor service records for hardware maintenance
Change management tickets categorized as maintenance activities
System maintenance schedule with completed-activity tracking
Equipment service logs for ancillary devices (printers, scanners, copiers)

commonArtifacts: System maintenance policy defining scope, frequency, and accountability
Maintenance records and work orders (hardware and software)
Patch management reports showing deployment status across systems
Vendor/manufacturer maintenance contracts and service records
Change management records classifying maintenance activities
Equipment maintenance schedule and completion tracking
System Security Plan section on maintenance procedures
commonGaps: Maintenance is performed but not documented — no records exist to demonstrate compliance
Patch management exists for servers but not for workstations, printers, or network equipment
No defined maintenance schedule — maintenance only happens reactively when something breaks
Vendor maintenance contracts exist but no internal records confirm the work was actually performed
Maintenance records cover IT assets but not ancillary devices that process or store CUI (multifunction printers, scanners)
Patching is performed ad hoc with no tracking of outstanding patches or patch age

title: System Maintenance Control
controlText: Provide controls on the tools, techniques, mechanisms, and personnel used to conduct system maintenance.
plainEnglish: Everything used to perform system maintenance — the tools, the techniques, the mechanisms, and the people — must be controlled. Maintenance tools are potential vectors for introducing malicious code into systems, either intentionally or unintentionally. The Assessment Guide calls out four distinct categories that assessors evaluate independently: tools (hardware/software diagnostic equipment, packet sniffers), techniques (procedures and methods), mechanisms (automated scripts, scheduled jobs, administrative portals), and personnel (who is authorized to perform maintenance and how they are supervised). Controls may include approving and inventorying tools, access-restricting maintenance entry points, and ensuring only authorized individuals perform maintenance activities.

objectives.text: tools used to conduct system maintenance are controlled
objectives.whatToLookFor: An approved inventory of maintenance tools with access restrictions ensuring only authorized personnel can use them. Assessors look for evidence that tools are vetted before use (scanned for malware, version-controlled), that unauthorized tools cannot be introduced, and that the tool inventory is current. Diagnostic hardware and software — including packet sniffers, diagnostic USB drives, and vendor-provided utilities — should all be accounted for.
commonEvidence: Approved maintenance tool inventory with version and access controls
Tool check-out/check-in procedures
Scan or verification records confirming maintenance tools are malware-free before use
Access controls limiting maintenance tool storage locations to authorized personnel

objectives.text: techniques used to conduct system maintenance are controlled
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented, approved maintenance procedures and techniques — not ad hoc methods chosen by whoever is performing the work. Assessors look for standardized runbooks or procedures, access controls on maintenance documentation, and evidence that personnel follow approved techniques rather than improvising.
commonEvidence: Documented maintenance procedures and runbooks
Access-controlled maintenance documentation repository
Change management or work order templates specifying approved techniques
Audit records showing adherence to defined maintenance procedures

objectives.text: mechanisms used to conduct system maintenance are controlled
objectives.whatToLookFor: Access controls on automated maintenance mechanisms — scripts, scheduled jobs, administrative portals, console access points, and physical equipment panels. Assessors verify that these entry points require authentication, that access is restricted to authorized maintenance personnel, and that there is visibility into when and how these mechanisms are used.
commonEvidence: Access control configuration for administrative portals used during maintenance
Authentication requirements for local and remote console access
Scheduled task / cron job inventory with ownership and authorization records
Audit logs showing access to maintenance mechanisms with attribution

objectives.text: personnel used to conduct system maintenance are controlled
objectives.whatToLookFor: A defined list or process for authorizing maintenance personnel, with evidence that unauthorized individuals are supervised or escorted. Assessors look for access authorization records, vendor and contractor management procedures, and controls ensuring that only vetted individuals perform unsupervised maintenance on CUI systems.
commonEvidence: Authorized maintenance personnel list with approval records
Vendor and contractor access authorization procedures
Supervision or escort records for maintenance personnel without standing authorization
Temporary credential issuance records with expiration controls for short-term maintenance personnel

commonArtifacts: System maintenance policy covering tools, techniques, mechanisms, and personnel
Approved maintenance tool inventory with access and version controls
Documented maintenance procedures and runbooks
Authorized maintenance personnel list
Vendor and contractor access management records
Audit logs for maintenance tool use and administrative portal access
Temporary credential records for short-term maintenance visitors
commonGaps: No approved tool inventory — maintenance technicians bring in personal laptops or USB drives with no vetting
Maintenance techniques undocumented — each administrator uses their own approach with no standardization
Administrative portals and console access have no authentication beyond network-level access (no separate maintenance access controls)
Any IT staff member can perform maintenance on CUI systems — no formal authorization process for maintenance personnel
Vendor technicians given full administrative accounts rather than time-limited, scope-restricted temporary credentials
Maintenance tools not scanned for malware before connecting to systems — malware introduction risk unmitigated

title: Equipment Sanitization
controlText: Ensure equipment removed for off-site maintenance is sanitized of any CUI.
plainEnglish: Before any equipment leaves organizational facilities for off-site maintenance — whether for hardware repair, vendor service, or warranty replacement — it must be sanitized to ensure no CUI remains on it. Sanitization means making data access infeasible on the media: overwriting with fixed patterns, purging via degaussing or secure erase, or destroying the media entirely if sanitization is not possible. The Assessment Guide references NIST SP 800-88 as the authoritative guidance for selecting the appropriate sanitization method for a given media type. This is a single-objective control but has significant documentation requirements: assessors expect to see both a sanitization process and sanitization records showing it was actually followed for equipment that left the facility.

objectives.text: equipment to be removed from organizational spaces for off-site maintenance is sanitized of any CUI
objectives.whatToLookFor: A defined sanitization process for equipment leaving the facility for off-site maintenance, and records showing that process was followed for specific equipment. Records should identify the equipment, the sanitization method applied (referencing NIST 800-88 categories: clear, purge, destroy), the date, and the person who performed the sanitization. For storage arrays, hard drives, and laptops this is straightforward; for equipment where data removal is not technically feasible, the records should document the destruction action taken.
commonEvidence: Equipment sanitization procedure referencing NIST SP 800-88
Sanitization records with equipment identifier, method, date, and responsible party
Degaussing logs or secure-erase tool output records
Chain-of-custody documentation for equipment sent to vendor
Destruction certificates for equipment where sanitization was not feasible

commonArtifacts: Equipment sanitization policy and procedure (referencing NIST SP 800-88)
Sanitization records log with equipment ID, method, date, and personnel
Approved sanitization tool or method inventory (degaussing unit, secure erase software)
Vendor maintenance request process requiring sanitization sign-off before release
Chain-of-custody records for equipment shipped to off-site maintenance
Destruction certificates for media that could not be sanitized
commonGaps: No sanitization process defined — equipment is shipped to vendors with whatever data remains on it
Sanitization is performed but not documented — cannot demonstrate compliance to an assessor
Only hard drives sanitized; firmware, NVRAM, and solid-state storage in other components overlooked
Sanitization policy exists but applies only to disposal, not to maintenance handoffs — the off-site maintenance scenario is not covered
Technicians use 'delete files' or format commands rather than NIST 800-88 compliant sanitization methods
No chain-of-custody record between sanitization and handoff to vendor — gap in audit trail

title: Media Inspection
controlText: Check media containing diagnostic and test programs for malicious code before the media are used in organizational systems.
plainEnglish: Any media — USB drives, diagnostic software downloads, vendor-provided utilities, or test tools — that contain diagnostic or test programs must be scanned for malicious code before being connected to or installed on organizational systems. The Assessment Guide explicitly notes this extends SI.L1-3.14.2 (malicious code protection) and SI.L1-3.14.4 (update malicious code protection) specifically to the maintenance context, where vendor-supplied diagnostic tools are a common and often under-scrutinized attack vector. If malicious code is found during inspection, the incident must be handled per the IR program. The single objective is that the check happens — assessors want to see both a defined process and evidence of execution.

objectives.text: media containing diagnostic and test programs are checked for malicious code before being used in organizational systems that process, store, or transmit CUI
objectives.whatToLookFor: A defined procedure for scanning maintenance media before use, and records showing the scans were performed. Assessors look for evidence that the check is systematic (not optional), that it covers all categories of maintenance media (not just USB drives but also downloaded utilities, vendor FTP deliveries, and diagnostic software), and that hash verification is used where possible to confirm file integrity. For downloaded files, records of the antivirus scan output and hash verification against vendor-published values are the expected evidence.
commonEvidence: Media inspection procedure requiring malware scan before maintenance media use
Antivirus/antimalware scan records for maintenance tools and diagnostic utilities
Hash verification records confirming file integrity against vendor-published checksums
Incident records for any positive malware detections during media inspection
Maintenance records noting media inspection completion before tool use

commonArtifacts: Media inspection policy and procedure for maintenance diagnostic tools
Antivirus/antimalware scan logs for maintenance media
Hash verification records (comparing vendor-published checksums to downloaded files)
Incident response records for malware detected during media inspection
Maintenance work order requiring media inspection sign-off before tool use
commonGaps: Vendor-provided diagnostic utilities installed directly from vendor FTP with no malware scan
Media inspection only covers USB drives — downloaded software utilities not scanned
Antivirus scans performed but not documented — no records to demonstrate the check occurred
No hash verification performed — file integrity confirmation absent even when vendors publish checksums
Inspection policy exists for end-user file downloads but maintenance technicians are treated as an exception
Positive malware detection during media inspection not treated as an incident — handled informally without IR procedures

title: Nonlocal Maintenance
controlText: Require multifactor authentication to establish nonlocal maintenance sessions via external network connections and terminate such connections when nonlocal maintenance is complete.
plainEnglish: When maintenance is performed remotely — via an external network connection rather than from within the facility — multifactor authentication must be required to establish the session, and the connection must be terminated when the maintenance is complete. The Assessment Guide explicitly ties this to IA.L2-3.5.3 (MFA for network access) and five remote access controls (AC.L2-3.1.12 through AC.L2-3.1.15). This is not a new concept for most organizations, but the maintenance context matters: vendor remote support sessions, firewall management interfaces accessed from home, and remote desktop connections used by sysadmins conducting maintenance all fall under this control. Two objectives are assessed independently: MFA is used to establish the session, and the session is terminated when maintenance is complete.

objectives.text: multifactor authentication is used to establish nonlocal maintenance sessions via external network connections
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that MFA is required before any remote maintenance session can be established — not just for general remote access but specifically for maintenance interfaces (firewall management consoles, jump servers, out-of-band management, remote desktop). Assessors look at authentication logs and configuration evidence showing that MFA cannot be bypassed for maintenance sessions, including by privileged users and vendor technicians.
commonEvidence: MFA configuration records for remote maintenance interfaces (firewall management, jump servers, IPMI/BMC)
Authentication logs showing MFA challenges for remote maintenance sessions
Vendor remote access procedures requiring MFA before support session establishment
VPN configuration enforcing MFA for connections used for remote maintenance

objectives.text: nonlocal maintenance sessions established via external network connections are terminated when nonlocal maintenance is complete
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that remote maintenance sessions are not left open indefinitely after maintenance is complete — either through manual termination procedures documented in work orders, or through automated session timeout policies. Assessors look for session duration records showing connections are closed after maintenance, configuration evidence of session timeouts, and maintenance work order completion steps requiring session termination.
commonEvidence: Session termination records in maintenance work orders (logged-out, connection closed)
Session timeout configuration for remote maintenance interfaces
VPN or remote access logs showing session duration aligned with maintenance windows
Vendor remote access session logs with clear start and end times

commonArtifacts: Remote maintenance policy requiring MFA and session termination
MFA configuration documentation for remote maintenance interfaces
Authentication and session logs for remote maintenance connections
Maintenance work order template with session termination step
Vendor remote support session management procedures
VPN and remote access configuration showing MFA enforcement
commonGaps: MFA enforced for general VPN access but remote maintenance interfaces (IPMI, out-of-band management) are accessible without MFA
Vendor remote support sessions do not require MFA — vendors connect via shared credentials or single-factor access
Remote maintenance sessions left open after work is complete — no termination requirement enforced
Session timeouts configured but set at 8–24 hours, effectively allowing sessions to persist indefinitely
MFA used for initial VPN connection but the maintenance interface itself accepts single-factor authentication once on the network
No maintenance session records — cannot demonstrate whether sessions were terminated upon completion

title: Maintenance Personnel
controlText: Supervise the maintenance activities of maintenance personnel without required access authorization.
plainEnglish: When maintenance must be performed by individuals who do not hold the required access authorization for the systems they are working on — vendors, contractors, IT manufacturers, consultants, or systems integrators without prior vetting — their activities must be supervised. The Assessment Guide explicitly notes this applies to hardware and software maintenance on organizational systems (distinct from PE.L1-3.10.1 which covers physical plant maintenance). Organizations may issue temporary credentials with short-term expiration for these individuals rather than standing accounts, and should apply the principle of least privilege: access limited to only the systems necessary to complete the work. The single objective is that supervision occurs for unauthorized-access personnel — assessors look for both a defined supervision process and evidence it was followed.

objectives.text: maintenance personnel without required access authorization are supervised during maintenance activities
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that a supervision process exists for maintenance personnel without prior access authorization, and records showing the process was followed for specific maintenance events. Supervision may be physical (escorting vendor technicians through the data center) or logical (monitoring network activity, using screen-sharing observation). Assessors expect to see temporary credential records with expiration dates and scope limitations, and supervision logs or notes documenting the oversight that occurred.
commonEvidence: Supervision procedures for maintenance personnel without standing access authorization
Temporary account records with limited permissions and expiration dates
Visitor or contractor escort logs for on-site maintenance events
Network monitoring records during vendor maintenance sessions
Maintenance completion sign-off confirming access was revoked post-maintenance
Service vendor access authorization forms with scope and duration limits

commonArtifacts: Maintenance personnel authorization policy and supervision procedures
Temporary credential issuance records (account, scope, expiration, requestor)
Contractor and vendor access authorization forms
Visitor/escort logs for on-site maintenance visits
Access revocation records showing temporary accounts disabled after maintenance
Network or session monitoring logs for vendor maintenance activities
commonGaps: Vendor technicians given the same full-access service account used by internal IT — no scope restriction or expiration
Supervision process defined in policy but no records showing supervision actually occurred during specific maintenance events
Temporary accounts created for vendors but never disabled after maintenance is complete
No visitor or escort log for on-site vendor maintenance — physical supervision undocumented
Vendor remote maintenance sessions not monitored — technician activity during remote session is unobserved
Access authorization process only applies to new employees; external maintenance contractors bypass it entirely
